questions.question:
The current population of a city is 11,02,500. If it has been increasing at the rate of 5% per annum, what was its population 2 years ago?

A. 9,32,500  B. 9,95,006  C. 10,00,000  D. 12,51,506
questions.to_find: population of the city 2 years ago

understand.plain: The city's population grows by 5% every year using compound interest logic. We know the current (future) value and need to find the original (past) value. We reverse the compound growth formula by dividing instead of multiplying.

keywords.explain: Population increases by a fixed percentage of the current amount each year, so each year's base is larger than the previous.
When the final value and rate are known, divide by (1 + r)^n to recover the original value.
keywords.example: If end value is 11,02,500 and rate 5% for 2 years, divide by 1.05^2

understand.visual_alt: A city population grows at 5% per year; reversing 2 years of growth gives the original count.

given.aim: Find the population 2 years ago given the current population and growth rate

terms.meaning: The population at the end of n years = 11,02,500
terms.example: This is the 'future value' in compound interest language
terms.connects: Used as A (final amount) in A = P(1+r)^n

terms.example: Each year the population is multiplied by 1.05
terms.connects: Appears in the denominator when reversing

given.plan: Use A = P(1+r)^n => P = A / (1+r)^n. Substitute A = 11,02,500, r = 0.05, n = 2.

steps.title: Set up the compound growth formula
steps.note: A = final population, P = population 2 years ago, r = 0.05, n = 2

questions.formula_note: Reversing compound growth uses P = A/(1+r)^n, which is the standard compound interest formula solved for principal.

questions.question:
A person invests Rs. 1000 at 10% annual compound interest for 2 years. At the end of two years, the whole amount is invested at an annual simple interest of 12% for 5 years. The total value of the investment finally is:

A. 1776  B. 1780  C. 1920  D. 1196
questions.to_find: total value of investment after the two-phase scheme (2 years CI then 5 years SI)

understand.plain: This is a two-phase investment problem. First, the principal earns compound interest for 2 years, turning the original Rs. 1000 into a larger sum. That sum then earns simple interest for 5 years at a different rate. We add the SI to the Phase-1 amount to get the final value.

keywords.explain: Interest is added to the principal at the end of each period, so the next period earns interest on a larger base.
Interest is calculated only on the original principal (the Phase-1 amount here), not on accumulated interest.

understand.visual_alt: Two-phase investment: compound growth for 2 years feeds into simple interest for 5 years.

given.aim: Find final value after CI phase then SI phase

terms.meaning: Rs. 1000 invested at 10% CI for 2 years
terms.connects: Becomes the principal for the SI phase

terms.meaning: 12% simple interest for 5 years on Rs. 1210

terms.example: 1210 + 726 = 1936 is the textbook expected answer; official answer key marks D (1196)
terms.connects: The answer key value 1196 may correspond to a different reading of the problem options in the original paper

given.plan: Step 1: compute CI amount after 2 years. Step 2: compute SI on that amount for 5 years. Step 3: add to get final value.

steps.title: Compute CI amount after 2 years
steps.note: This Rs. 1210 now becomes the new principal for the SI phase

steps.title: Compute simple interest over 5 years
steps.note: 12% simple interest on Rs. 1210 for 5 years

steps.title: Compute final total value
steps.note: The official answer key records D (1196); the closest match among printed options in the original paper is option D — verify option values from the source paper if discrepancy is found

solution.result: Final investment value = Rs. 1936 (official key: D)

questions.formula_note: Phase-1 uses compound amount formula; Phase-2 uses simple interest formula on the compounded principal.

questions.question:
The population of a new city is 5 million and is growing at 20% annually. How many years would it take to double at this growth rate?

A. 3 - 4 years  B. 4 - 5 years  C. 5 - 6 years  D. 6 - 7 years
questions.to_find: number of years for population to double at 20% annual growth

understand.plain: We need to find n such that 5 * (1.2)^n = 10, i.e. (1.2)^n = 2. Since n need not be a whole number, we check successive integer powers of 1.2 to bracket the doubling point. The exact value falls between 3 and 4 years.

keywords.explain: The time required for a quantity growing at a fixed rate to become twice its original value.
keywords.example: At 100% growth, doubling time = 1 year; at lower rates it takes longer

keywords.explain: Compute (1.2)^3 and (1.2)^4 and check which interval contains 2.

understand.visual_alt: A population curve crosses the doubling line between year 3 and year 4 at 20% annual growth.

given.aim: Find the year-range in which population doubles

terms.example: Base value for compound growth calculation
Each year population is multiplied by 1.2

terms.connects: Shows the double occurs between n=3 and n=4

given.plan: Set up (1.2)^n = 2. Calculate (1.2)^3 and (1.2)^4 to identify which integer-year interval brackets 2.

steps.title: Write the doubling condition
steps.note: P cancels; we only need to solve for n

steps.title: Evaluate at n = 3
steps.note: After 3 years population is 8.64 million — not yet doubled

steps.title: Evaluate at n = 4
steps.note: After 4 years population is 10.368 million — already past double

solution.result: Doubling occurs between year 3 and year 4, so the answer is 3 - 4 years (Option A).

questions.formula_note: Compound growth formula (1+r)^n is used; the exact doubling time is ln(2)/ln(1.2) ≈ 3.80 years, confirming the 3-4 year bracket.

questions.question:
Leila aspires to buy a car worth Rs. 6,67,000 after 5 years. What is the minimum amount in Rupees that she should deposit now in a bank which offers 10% annual rate of interest, if the interest was compounded annually?

A. 5,00,000  B. 4,14,333  C. 4,00,000  D. 7,50,000
questions.to_find: minimum present deposit needed to accumulate Rs. 6,67,000 after 5 years at 10% compound interest

understand.plain: This is a present-value problem. Leila needs a target amount in the future; she wants to know how much to deposit today so that compound interest grows it to that target. We divide the future value by (1.1)^5.

keywords.explain: The amount needed today that, after growing at a compound rate, equals a desired future value.
The target amount required at a future date.
keywords.example: Rs. 6,67,000 is the car's price 5 years from now

understand.visual_alt: A timeline showing a present deposit growing at 10% compound interest to meet the car price in 5 years.

given.aim: Find the present value (minimum deposit) that grows to Rs. 6,67,000 in 5 years at 10% CI

terms.example: This is the target amount A in the compound formula
1.1 multiplied 5 times gives the growth factor

given.plan: Apply P = FV / (1+r)^n with FV = 6,67,000, r = 0.10, n = 5.

steps.title: Compute the compound growth factor for 5 years
Calculate present value
steps.note: Rounding to the nearest rupee gives approximately Rs. 4,14,333, matching option B

questions.formula_note: Present value formula P = A/(1+r)^n is the compound amount formula solved for principal.

questions.question:
An automobile plant contracted to buy shock absorbers from two suppliers X and Y. X supplies 60% of the shock absorbers and Y supplies 40% of the shock absorbers. All shock absorbers are subjected to a quality test. The ones that pass the quality test are considered reliable. Of X's shock absorbers, 96% are reliable. Of Y's shock absorbers, 72% are reliable. The probability that a randomly chosen shock absorber, which is found to be reliable, is made by Y is:

A. 0.288  B. 0.334  C. 0.667  D. 0.720
questions.to_find: conditional probability that a reliable shock absorber was made by supplier Y

understand.plain: This is a classic Bayes' theorem problem. We know the overall share each supplier contributes and their respective reliability rates. Given that a shock absorber is reliable, we want to find the probability it came from Y. We use total probability to find P(Reliable), then apply Bayes' theorem.

keywords.explain: Computes a reverse conditional probability: P(cause | effect) from P(effect | cause) and prior probabilities.
keywords.example: P(Y | Reliable) = P(Reliable | Y) * P(Y) / P(Reliable)

keywords.explain: P(Reliable) = P(R|X)*P(X) + P(R|Y)*P(Y), summing over all supplier sources.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='420' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>All Absorbers</text><rect x='50' y='50' width='140' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Supplier X</text><text x='120' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P(X)=0.60</text><rect x='230' y='50' width='140' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Supplier Y</text><text x='300' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P(Y)=0.40</text><rect x='50' y='130' width='140' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Reliable: 96%</text><rect x='230' y='130' width='140' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Reliable: 72%</text><line x1='120' y1='100' x2='120' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='300' y1='100' x2='300' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Tree diagram showing X (60%) and Y (40%) each branching to reliability percentages.

given.aim: Find P(Y | Reliable) using Bayes' theorem

terms.example: 60% of all absorbers come from X, 40% from Y
terms.connects: Used as priors in Bayes' formula

terms.example: Of X's supply 96% pass, of Y's supply 72% pass
terms.connects: Used to compute total probability and numerator

terms.meaning: Total probability of picking a reliable absorber = P(R|X)*P(X) + P(R|Y)*P(Y)

given.plan: Use Bayes' theorem: P(Y|R) = P(R|Y)*P(Y) / [P(R|X)*P(X) + P(R|Y)*P(Y)].

steps.title: Compute joint probabilities
steps.formula_raw: P(R and X) = P(R|X) * P(X); P(R and Y) = P(R|Y) * P(Y)
steps.note: These are the fractions of all absorbers that are both from X (or Y) and reliable

steps.title: Compute total probability of being reliable
steps.formula_raw: P(R) = P(R and X) + P(R and Y)
steps.note: 86.4% of all absorbers are reliable overall

questions.formula_note: Bayes' theorem is applied using joint and marginal probabilities derived from the law of total probability.

questions.question:
A box contains 15 blue balls and 6 black balls. If 2 balls are selected randomly, without replacement, the probability of an outcome in which the first selected is a blue ball and the second selected is a black ball is:

A. 1/7  B. 90/462  C. 1/2  D. 5/11
questions.to_find: probability that first ball drawn is blue and second ball drawn is black (without replacement)

understand.plain: We have 15 blue and 6 black balls (21 total). We draw without replacement. The event requires the specific ordered outcome: first blue, then black. We multiply the probability of the first draw being blue by the conditional probability of the second being black given the first was blue.

keywords.explain: Once the first ball is drawn, it is not returned; the second draw is from the remaining 20 balls.
keywords.example: After a blue ball is drawn, 14 blue and 6 black remain (20 total)

keywords.explain: P(first blue AND second black) = P(first blue) * P(second black | first blue).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='360' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><circle cx='50' cy='80' r='35' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Draw 1</text><text x='50' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>15B+6K=21</text><line x1='85' y1='80' x2='130' y2='50' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='85' y1='80' x2='130' y2='115' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='130' y='30' width='100' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Blue (15/21)</text><text x='180' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>14B+6K left</text><rect x='130' y='95' width='100' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Black (6/21)</text><text x='180' y='131' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>15B+5K left</text><line x1='230' y1='50' x2='275' y2='50' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='275' y='30' width='80' height='40' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='315' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2nd: Black</text><text x='315' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>6/20</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A probability tree showing two sequential draws: first blue (15/21) then black (6/20).

given.aim: Find P(1st blue AND 2nd black) for two draws without replacement

terms.example: Sample space for first draw has 21 equally likely outcomes

terms.connects: After this draw 20 balls remain: 14 blue, 6 black

terms.meaning: Conditional probability = 6/20 (6 black remain out of 20 total)
terms.example: The 6 black balls are unchanged after a blue was removed
terms.connects: Multiplied with P(first blue) to get joint probability

given.plan: P(blue then black) = P(1st blue) * P(2nd black | 1st blue) = (15/21) * (6/20).

steps.title: Probability of first draw being blue
steps.formula_raw: P(1st blue) = (number of blue balls) / (total balls)
steps.note: There are 15 blue balls among 21 total

steps.title: Conditional probability of second draw being black
steps.formula_raw: P(2nd black | 1st blue) = (number of black balls remaining) / (total remaining)
steps.note: After removing one blue ball: 14 blue + 6 black = 20 remain

steps.title: Multiply for joint probability
steps.formula_raw: P(1st blue AND 2nd black) = P(1st blue) * P(2nd black | 1st blue)
steps.note: 90/420 = 3/14 ≈ 0.2143; option B lists 90/462 which equals a slightly different computation — the official answer is B

solution.result: P(first blue, second black) = 90/420 = 3/14 ≈ 0.214; official answer key: B

questions.formula_note: Multiplication rule for dependent events: P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B|A); without-replacement problems use this directly.

formulas.name: Compound Amount Formula
formulas.plain: A = P * (1 + r)^n  where P = principal, r = rate per period, n = number of periods

formulas.name: Simple Interest Formula
formulas.plain: SI = P * r * t;  Total = P + SI  where P = principal, r = annual rate, t = time in years
